executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment continues its post-pandemic normalization trend, with 74.9 million enrollees as of February 2026 representing sustained declines across major states following the end of continuous enrollment protections. Year-over-year enrollment decreases ranged from North Carolina's stable enrollment (0.0% change) to Michigan's significant reduction (-7.6%), with most states experiencing 2-5% declines. Texas ranks third nationally with 4.0 million enrollees, showing a moderate 3.8% year-over-year decrease that aligns with national patterns. Total program expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a national average per-enrollee cost of $2,883. Texas demonstrates relatively efficient spending at $3,014 per enrollee—higher than the national average but substantially lower than comparable large states like New York ($3,460) and Pennsylvania ($4,363), while managing the fourth-largest state program by total expenditure ($12.0 billion).

Quality performance reveals significant interstate variation, with national average scores of 65.6 out of 100 and a concerning 15 states performing below the 60-point threshold. Texas achieves a moderate quality score of 62 points, ranking 30th nationally—a position that warrants attention given the state's program size and relatively efficient per-enrollee spending. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%, covering nearly 3.9 million enrollees and positioning Texas as a leader in care coordination infrastructure. Recent federal regulatory activity indicates heightened oversight focus, with multiple CMS initiatives targeting managed care state-directed payments, provider enrollment moratoria, and community engagement requirements that may impact state operations in the coming year.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.0 million enrollees, demonstrating relatively controlled enrollment decline (-3.8% year-over-year) that falls between the modest decreases seen in peer southern states like North Carolina (0.0%) and Florida (-4.2%), but outperforms larger states like California (-6.8%) and Michigan (-7.6%). The state's fiscal performance shows notable efficiency with per-enrollee spending of $3,014—significantly below the national average of $2,883 and well under expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,363) and Ohio ($3,584). This cost control becomes particularly impressive when considering Texas serves nearly 4 million beneficiaries without Medicaid expansion, suggesting effective program administration despite serving a more limited but potentially higher-acuity eligible population.

However, Texas faces persistent quality challenges that demand attention, scoring 62/100 on national quality measures and ranking 30th nationally—a concerning position that places it closer to low-performing southern non-expansion peers like Arkansas (54) and South Carolina (53) than to national leaders. The state's strength lies in its managed care infrastructure, with 93.3% penetration率 substantially exceeding the national average of 83.2%, indicating sophisticated care coordination capabilities that aren't yet translating to quality outcomes. Key areas to monitor include potential federal regulatory changes around community engagement requirements and state-directed payments, which could significantly impact Texas's program structure, while the state's robust procurement activity suggests ongoing efforts to enhance program performance through vendor partnerships and technological improvements.

alerts.title: High Per-Enrollee Spending in PA and MA
alerts.description: Pennsylvania ($4,363) and Massachusetts ($4,221) show per-enrollee spending significantly above the national average of $2,883, indicating potential cost management issues.

alerts.title: Very Low Quality Scores in Southern States
alerts.description: Five states (MS, OK, AL, SC, AR) have quality scores below 55, with Mississippi at critically low 48, indicating severe care quality concerns.

alerts.title: Florida: Low Spending with Poor Quality Outcomes
alerts.description: Florida has the lowest per-enrollee spending at $2,036 but this may correlate with inadequate care access given national quality concerns.

alerts.title: Nationwide Provider Enrollment Moratorium Active
alerts.description: CMS has announced temporary nationwide moratoria on Medicare and Medicaid provider enrollment, which may impact network adequacy.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score lags at 62/100, ranking #30 nationally
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid quality performance (62/100) sits below national average of 65.6 and ranks 30th out of 50 states. This places Texas in the moderate tier, significantly behind top performers like MA (81) and MN (80).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts federal funding, regulatory scrutiny, and contract renewals. Poor performance could trigger CMS interventions or jeopardize future contract opportunities.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Federal enrollment moratorium signals imminent regulatory changes
executiveInsights.summary: Multiple Federal Register entries show CMS announcing nationwide temporary moratoria on Medicare/Medicaid enrollments in May 2026. This indicates significant federal policy shifts affecting program operations.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment freezes could fundamentally alter Texas Medicaid operations, revenue projections, and system requirements. Accenture needs to prepare HHSC for potential operational pivots.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review Federal Register moratoria details and brief HHSC on implications

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining faster than peer states at -3.8% YoY
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.8% year-over-year to 3.997M, outpacing declines in similar large states like NY (-2.2%) and PA (-2.9%). Only NC maintained flat enrollment (0.0%).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Accelerated enrollment decline suggests potential eligibility redetermination issues or operational challenges that could impact federal compliance and program sustainability.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment trend analysis to HHSC eligibility team

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs exceed national average by $130 annually
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $3,014 per Medicaid enrollee versus the national average of $2,883, representing a 4.5% premium. This totals approximately $520M in above-average spending annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs indicate potential inefficiencies or service delivery issues that could attract federal scrutiny and impact budget negotiations with the legislature.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost driver analysis with HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: Managed care rule changes targeting state directed payments effective 2026
executiveInsights.summary: Federal Register shows new CMS rules on Medicaid managed care state directed payments effective in 2026. Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration, making this highly relevant.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: With nearly all Texas Medicaid enrollees in managed care, payment rule changes could significantly impact contractor relationships, financial flows, and compliance requirements.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care payment rule changes with HHSC contracts team

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.8% YoY, indicating potential unwinding challenges that could stress system operations.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop retention analytics and automated eligibility verification solutions to help HHSC manage unwinding.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality improvement gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating opportunity for performance enhancement initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality analytics platform to elevate Texas from moderate to high-performer tier.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas' 93.3% managed care penetration signals potential upcoming procurement opportunities in a $12B+ market.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Monitor HHSC procurement portal and position for managed care contract modernization opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal regulatory shifts
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple CMS rule changes around state directed payments and community engagement create compliance complexity.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish regulatory monitoring center to help Texas navigate changing federal requirements proactively.

riskOpportunity.label: Cost efficiency pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas spends $3,014 per enrollee versus national average of $2,883, suggesting budget optimization needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop predictive cost management tools to identify savings opportunities without compromising care quality.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio monitoring increases audit risk but validates need for compliance technology.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance automation offerings to differentiate from competitors in risk-averse environment.

riskOpportunity.label: Provider enrollment moratorium
riskOpportunity.description: Nationwide temporary provider enrollment restrictions create network adequacy challenges requiring innovative solutions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Design provider network optimization tools to maximize existing capacity during enrollment restrictions.
